topic: Embedded - no_std with the alloc Crate

turns.content: I'm getting a linker error when I try to compile my proof-of-conceit. The message says undefined reference to 'core::panicking::panic_impl' and it seems like I forgot something about the global allocator in no_std environments.

That looks like you're missing a basic requirement for #![no_std] builds. In Rust, when you disable the standard library, the panic implementation isn't automatically available unless you explicitly provide one. You need to add extern crate alloc; to your crate root so that the core::panicking module gets linked properly without relying on std's runtime hooks.

I added extern crate alloc but it still fails. I thought maybe I needed to manually implement panic, or perhaps I misunderstood how the global allocator connects to Vec and String operations in this context?

You're right to suspect the connection between allocation and panics. In no_std land with a custom heap allocator like embedded-alloc, every data structure that owns memory—Vec, String, HashMap—must use your allocator's implementation of Drop and Panicking traits. The extern crate alloc; line is mandatory because it exports the core module which contains panic_impl. Without it, the compiler cannot find symbols for things like Vec::new or String::from_utf8, even if you've defined a heap_allocator.

Okay so I added the extern crate and now I can compile basic types but still have runtime issues when my program tries to allocate large buffers. It seems like embedded-alloc isn't being picked up correctly in the build script?

The issue is likely that you're not explicitly telling Cargo which allocator to use during compilation. Even with extern crate alloc;, if your Cargo.toml doesn't specify [build-dependencies] or uses cfg-if checks, the fallback might still be using std's allocator. You should declare embedded-alloc as a build-dependency in your Cargo.toml and ensure your main.rs explicitly imports it so that the heap_allocator trait is resolved before any Vec or String operations occur.

I updated Cargo.toml with [build-dependencies] but now I get a different error. It says 'no rule matches' for some function call. Is this because my custom allocator isn't implementing the required traits correctly?

Yes, that's exactly it. Your heap_allocator implementation must fully implement Drop and Panicking traits to replace std's behavior. If you're missing a method or using an incorrect trait name from embedded-alloc, the compiler won't know how to handle memory cleanup during drop. For example, if your allocator doesn't correctly implement core::panicking::Panic, then any panic attempt will fail at compile time because there's no target for the signal.

I checked my code and it looks correct. But I still can't get Vec to work with embedded-alloc without errors. Is there something specific about how the alloc crate interacts with the standard library types that I'm missing?

The key is understanding that extern crate alloc; only brings in core, not std's internal structs directly. When you use Vec or String, they rely on the allocator trait implemented by your heap_allocator. If your implementation doesn't correctly implement Drop and Panicking for the specific version of embedded-alloc you're using, those types won't compile. You might also need to ensure your build script generates the correct cfg flags so that no_std paths are taken instead of std ones.

Ah, I think I see it now. But wait, earlier you said extern crate alloc; is enough for panic_impl. Why do I still need this extra setup? Isn't the global allocator supposed to be automatic once I declare it?

You're catching a subtle distinction here. The extern crate alloc; line provides access to core::panicking but doesn't automatically configure your heap_allocator's interaction with Vec and String. Those types still need the custom Drop and Panicking traits wired up correctly in your allocator implementation. It's not just about linking the symbols; it's about ensuring every allocation path through your code respects the embedded-alloc contract.

topic: Embedded - no_std with the alloc Crate

turns.content: I've been away from Rust for five years and I'm trying to get back into embedded development. The book says I should use #![no_std] with extern crate alloc; but isn't this still running code on the heap? So when I try to use Vec or String in a no_std context, doesn that mean there's still an implicit allocator somewhere?

You've hit exactly the right spot. When you write #![no_std], you're telling the compiler not to link against the standard library, which means no global heap is automatically available for things like Vec or String. The alloc crate provides a minimal allocator that gives you access to these types without requiring a full OS environment, but it's still an external dependency you must explicitly include. So yes, using Vec or String with extern crate alloc; does mean there's a heap allocator running in your binary, just one provided by the crate itself rather than the system.

Okay so I understand that extern crate alloc gives me access to Vec and String, but what happens if my microcontroller doesn't have enough RAM for a full allocator? Can I just drop down to bare metal without any heap at all?

That's the next level of embedded Rust. If your target has very limited resources, you can indeed move beyond alloc and use crates like embedded-alloc which provide a heap allocator specifically designed for constrained environments with minimal overhead. These crates implement their own allocation logic that works even when you have no standard library features available. However, if you truly need zero heap usage, you'd be writing everything as static arrays or using panic! to signal errors instead of allocating dynamically.
So I'm confused about the distinction between just having no_std versus actually needing a custom allocator. Can you explain why someone would choose embedded-alloc over the standard alloc crate?

The main difference is efficiency and memory footprint. The standard alloc crate provides a basic heap allocator that works fine for many projects, but it might include unnecessary features or dependencies if you're working on very small embedded systems. Crates like embedded-alloc are built specifically to minimize the code size and RAM usage by providing lightweight allocation strategies tailored for constrained hardware. This is important because every byte counts when you're writing firmware for a microcontroller with limited flash and SRAM.
Got it, but what about safety? If I'm using Vec or String with an external allocator, how do I prevent buffer overflows since there's no compiler-enforced bounds checking like in std?

You're right to be concerned. When you use Vec or String without the standard library, you lose some of its automatic safety guarantees. The allocator itself doesn't enforce bounds checking, so it's up to your code to manage memory carefully. You can still get some safety by using traits like SafeDrop for cleanup and careful error handling with panic! when things go wrong. But for critical systems where security matters, you might prefer avoiding dynamic allocation altogether in favor of fixed-size buffers or manual memory management.
That makes sense. So if I'm working on a project that needs to store large amounts of data dynamically, should I stick with the standard alloc crate or look into more advanced heap allocators?

If your application requires dynamic storage of substantial data, starting with the standard alloc crate is usually a good choice because it's well-tested and widely supported. However, if you later discover performance or memory constraints become an issue, you can swap in more sophisticated heap allocators like embedded-alloc or others that offer better fragmentation management or smaller code footprints. The key is to profile your system early to know whether the basic allocator will suffice or if a custom solution is needed.
One more thing, I noticed some tutorials mention using Box instead of Vec for certain cases in no_std environments. What's the advantage there?

Box is useful when you need heap-allocated data but want to own it directly rather than manage a container like Vec. It allows you to store pointers to dynamically allocated resources, which can be handy for creating small pools of objects or managing ownership chains in complex systems. However, Box still relies on an allocator behind the scenes, so if your goal is truly zero heap usage, you'd need to avoid Box entirely and use static allocations instead.
I think I'm getting clearer on when to use which approach. But what about error handling? If my allocator runs out of space in no_std, how do I handle that gracefully without panicking?

In a no_std context without the standard library's panic! mechanism, you need to implement custom error handling. You can create your own Result-like types and use traits like std::error::Error or similar interfaces that don't rely on panics. Many embedded projects define custom enums for allocation failures and return them through function signatures so callers can check conditions explicitly instead of relying on unwinding the stack.
Thanks, this helps a lot with understanding how to balance flexibility and constraints in my next embedded project. I'll start by trying extern crate alloc; and see if I need to switch later.

That's exactly the right approach. Start simple with the standard allocator, profile your system for memory usage and performance, then consider swapping in a more specialized heap allocator like embedded-alloc only when you identify specific bottlenecks. Good luck with your return to Rust development!

topic: Embedded - no_std with the alloc Crate

turns.content: I've been away from Rust for five years and I'm trying to fix a CI failure on our embedded project. It seems like my build is complaining about missing allocations even though I have the alloc crate in Cargo.toml, but when I try to use a Vec it fails with an error about no global allocator found.

Hey there! Welcome back. That's actually a very common gotcha for people coming from std environments. The issue isn't that the crate is missing; it's that you can't just add extern crate alloc to your #![no_std] module and expect it to magically create a global allocator for you. Think of the standard library's memory management like having a dedicated office building with a manager who always knows where everything is kept, whereas no_std is more like working in an open field without any buildings or roads, so if you try to build a house (a Vec) there, you need to lay out your own foundation first. The alloc crate provides the blueprint for that foundation but doesn't automatically pour the concrete.
Ah okay, so I need to manually implement the allocator trait? But what exactly does that mean in terms of code structure?

Exactly. You have two main paths here depending on your needs. The first is using the standard alloc crate which provides a simple heap-based implementation that works for many small projects, but it has limitations like no bounds checking and less safety features compared to std::vec::Vec. If you need something more robust or specific to embedded memory constraints, you'd look at crates like embedded-alloc which offer better integration with static analysis tools. The key difference is that in #![no_std] you must explicitly write a function implementing the Allocator trait that your runtime will call when you try to allocate memory.
Can you show me what that implementation looks like? I want to make sure I'm structuring my code correctly so it doesn't break during compilation.

Sure. You'd define a global function or a module-level trait implementation that takes care of the allocation logic. For example, you might create a simple allocator using raw pointers and a heap buffer like [u8; HEAP_SIZE] to manage your memory layout manually. The important thing is ensuring your #![no_std] directive matches where this allocator is defined so the compiler knows not to use std's internal mechanisms.
Wait, I think I misunderstood earlier. So if I'm using Vec in no_std context, does it automatically become a heap allocation even with my custom allocator?

Yes, once you've properly wired your custom allocator to the runtime system, any collection type that requires memory—like Vec or String—will use your allocator instead of std's internal one. But remember that even with a global allocator, these collections still manage their own heap internally, so they won't automatically handle stack overflow scenarios unless you add extra safeguards.
That makes sense now. One more thing though—does this mean I can never use Vec again without writing all these manual allocation functions?

Not at all. You can still use Vec and String, but they will route through your custom allocator instead of std's. The syntax remains the same, only the underlying implementation changes to respect #![no_std] constraints.

topic: Embedded - no_std with the alloc Crate

turns.content: I pasted this error message: 'cannot determine the type of this because it is not marked as const' and I'm getting a linker error when trying to use Vec in my embedded code. What went wrong?

The issue stems from how you're handling heap allocation without a global allocator. When compiling for no_std environments, using standard types like Vec or String requires an active memory allocator because they need to track their internal buffers. If your crate is marked with #![no_std] but lacks the extern crate alloc; directive, the compiler assumes there's no runtime memory management available and rejects code that dynamically allocates on the heap. You must explicitly provide a global allocator via the alloc crate or use an alternative like embedded-alloc to satisfy the type system's expectations for heap operations.

I added extern crate alloc; but now I'm getting 'no global allocator found' even though I referenced it in main. Is that because extern crate is deprecated? Should I be using #![feature(global_allocator)?

You are correct to question the extern crate approach, as that syntax was indeed removed in Rust 2015 and replaced by the modern #![feature(allocator)] or simply relying on the no_std environment's implicit handling. However, the real problem here is likely that your main function isn't actually using any heap allocation that triggers the allocator requirement. The error 'no global allocator found' appears only when you attempt to use types like Vec or String without providing a way for them to grow their internal buffers at runtime. If you haven't instantiated these types in your code, the compiler might not require an allocator even if you have #![no_std].
Wait, I just realized I am using String in my error messages throughout the app. So why did that work before? Did I accidentally enable std somewhere?

If your code previously compiled without issues despite having no_std flags, you likely had a different setup where either a global allocator was already present from another crate dependency or you were inadvertently using some form of standard library features that implicitly brought in an allocator. The modern approach for embedded systems requires explicitly choosing between the built-in alloc crate which provides a simple heap allocator, or switching to embedded-alloc which is designed specifically for constrained environments where you might not have enough memory for a full global allocator. You need to decide whether your target device supports a standard heap or if you are working with extremely limited resources.
I tried embedding embedded-alloc but now I can't use Vec anymore. Is there really no way to get dynamic arrays in this environment? Or do I have to manually manage memory?

You cannot use standard Vec or String types without a valid heap allocator present, because these types are designed to grow dynamically and require tracking their internal buffer sizes at runtime. If you switch to embedded-alloc, you lose the ability to use Vec directly unless you wrap your data structures in ways that satisfy the allocator's requirements. The solution is to either implement a custom allocator that provides the necessary global allocation interface or manually manage raw pointers and memory blocks yourself using unsafe code blocks where appropriate for low-level control.
I tried implementing my own allocator but it keeps failing at compile time with 'cannot satisfy trait bounds'. What specific traits am I missing?

To implement a custom heap allocator in no_std environments, you must provide the core trait implementations that allow your allocator to interact with the standard library's memory management expectations. Specifically, you need to implement the GlobalAlloc trait which defines methods like alloc, realloc, and free for your custom allocation logic. Additionally, if you are using types like Vec or String, they also require specific trait bounds on their internal buffers to ensure safe memory access patterns.

I looked at the documentation but it says I need to implement these traits in a special way. Can I just use the default allocator and only replace it when needed? Or does this require a complete rewrite?

You cannot simply override the default allocator partially; if you are using #![no_std], the entire runtime must be built from scratch or with specific crates designed for that environment. The standard library's global allocator is not available in no_std contexts by default, so you must either provide a replacement allocator entirely or use one of the pre-built options like embedded-alloc which handles the setup automatically. There is no partial override mechanism; you must choose between using a full custom allocator implementation or adopting an existing solution designed for embedded systems.
I'm confused about when exactly to drop the global allocator from my code. Does removing it break everything? I want to keep some std features but not all of them.

Removing a global allocator does not necessarily break your entire application, but it will prevent any dynamic allocation operations unless you provide an alternative mechanism. In no_std environments, you can selectively enable or disable certain standard library features by using conditional compilation attributes or specific feature flags when building your crate. However, if you remove the allocator entirely without providing a replacement, then any code attempting to use Vec, String, or other heap-based types will fail at compile time because those types fundamentally require an active memory management system.
Okay so I need to either pick embedded-alloc or write my own. But what if I want to mix and match? Can I have some parts of my code use std::vec and others use raw pointers?

You cannot mix standard Vec types with no global allocator present because those types inherently require the memory management system that provides them. If you want to support both dynamic arrays and low-level manual memory management, you must provide a complete alternative allocator for the entire runtime or ensure your code paths are separated using conditional compilation so that certain sections of your program only execute when an appropriate allocator is available.
I think I understand now but I'm still worried about safety. If I use embedded-alloc and then accidentally dereference a pointer incorrectly, will the system catch it? Is this really safe?

Using embedded-alloc or any custom allocator does not automatically provide runtime checks for unsafe operations; these allocators are designed to work in environments where performance and memory constraints are critical. Safety depends entirely on your code adhering to Rust's ownership rules and avoiding invalid pointer manipulations. While the allocator itself manages memory efficiently, it will not catch logic errors like double frees or buffer overflows unless you explicitly add additional safety checks using macros or runtime assertions.
